_pass: Pass 1 + worked examples v2 + LA trim + apps + extra exercises + exercises unify v2
title: Theoretical and experimental probability
deck: Theoretical probability comes from the structure of the experiment; experimental probability comes from the data of running it. The Law of Large Numbers says that as trials accumulate, the two converge.
objectives: Compute both theoretical and experimental probability for compound independent events, compare the two, and describe how they converge as the number of trials increases (the Law of Large Numbers).

content.text: Every probability statement in the chapters so far has been a theoretical statement, computed from the structure of the experiment itself. The probability that a fair coin lands heads is 1/2 because the sample space has two equally-likely outcomes and one of them is heads, full stop; we do not need to flip the coin even once to make the claim. But there is a second, distinct sense in which the word “probability” is used in scientific practice: the experimental probability, computed from the data of actually running the experiment many times and counting how often the event in question occurs.

Both quantities are useful, and they answer different questions. Theoretical probability is the prediction; experimental probability is the observation. The two are connected by a deep result called the Law of Large Numbers, which says (in plain English) that as the number of trials grows, the experimental probability of any event converges toward the theoretical probability. This closing section develops both definitions, the multiplication rule for compound events that makes theoretical computations possible at scale, and the long-run agreement that ties experimental observation to theoretical prediction.
6.6.1  Two definitions of probability

The theoretical probability of an event was defined in Section 6.1 as the ratio of favorable outcomes to total outcomes in the sample space, under the assumption that every outcome is equally likely. This is sometimes called the classical or a priori definition: we compute the probability before any data is collected, using only the structure of the experiment.

The experimental probability of the same event is defined differently: it is the proportion of trials, in a sequence of actual repetitions of the experiment, on which the event occurred. If a coin is flipped 200 times and lands heads on 109 of those flips, the experimental probability of heads in that data set is 109/200 = 0.545. The experimental probability depends on the data; a different run of 200 flips would almost certainly produce a different number.

Definition 6.7. The experimental probability of an event A, based on a sequence of n trials of the underlying experiment, is the ratio (number of trials on which A occurred) / n. The experimental probability depends on the particular sequence of trials; the theoretical probability does not.
6.6.2  The multiplication rule for independent events

Two events A and B are called independent when the occurrence of one does not change the probability of the other. Drawing a card from a deck and rolling a die are independent events; the card draw cannot affect the die. Flipping two coins is independent; the first coin's outcome cannot affect the second. By contrast, drawing two cards from the same deck without replacement is not independent; the first draw changes what is available for the second.

Theorem 6.2. (Multiplication rule for independent events.) If two events A and B are independent, then P(A ∩ B) = P(A) · P(B). The probability that both occur is the product of the individual probabilities.

The multiplication rule turns many compound probabilities into routine theoretical computations. The probability of rolling two even numbers on two dice is P(even on die 1) · P(even on die 2) = (1/2)(1/2) = 1/4. The probability of drawing an odd ball from {1, 2, 3} and a K-or-J card from {K, Q, J} is P(odd ball) · P(K or J) = (2/3)(2/3) = 4/9. In each case the structure of the experiment supplies the individual probabilities, and the multiplication rule supplies the compound one.
6.6.3  The Law of Large Numbers

The Law of Large Numbers ties the experimental probability of any event to its theoretical probability: as the number of trials grows without bound, the experimental probability of the event approaches the theoretical probability. The statement is not a guarantee about any single run of trials; even after a million trials there will be a small residual difference. The convergence is statistical, not algebraic; the gap shrinks but does not, in any single finite run, vanish.

content.caption: Figure 6.6. The Law of Large Numbers, plotted. The teal curve is the experimental probability of a single event as more and more trials accumulate; the dashed orange line is the theoretical probability. The experimental curve fluctuates wildly at small n and settles closer to the theoretical line as n grows.

content.text: Margin noteThe Law of Large Numbers was first proved (in the special case of binary outcomes) by Jacob Bernoulli around 1689 and published posthumously in Ars Conjectandi (1713). The general form was tightened in the twentieth century; the modern textbook versions distinguish between the “weak” and “strong” laws, which differ in the precise notion of convergence used.

The theoretical probability is the prediction. The experimental probability is the observation. The Law of Large Numbers is the promise that, given enough data, the observation will match the prediction.

Common mistakeClaiming that the Law of Large Numbers guarantees exact equality between the experimental and theoretical probabilities. It does not. The two probabilities approach each other as n grows, but a small residual difference is essentially always present. The correct verbal statement is “closer, not equal.” A related fallacy is the so-called gambler's fallacy, which holds that an unusual streak of one outcome makes the opposite outcome “due” on the next trial. The Law of Large Numbers makes no such claim; each future trial is governed by the theoretical probability independently of the past.

Example 6.6. Balls and cards: both probabilities.

A bag contains three balls numbered 1, 2, 3. A pack contains three cards labeled K, Q, J. On each trial, one ball is chosen and one card is drawn. After 70 trials, the outcomes (ball-card) are: 1K = 11, 2K = 8, 3K = 9, 1Q = 10, 2Q = 5, 3Q = 8, 1J = 8, 2J = 5, 3J = 6. Find the experimental and theoretical probability that the ball is odd and the card is either K or J.

Solution.

Step 1. Experimental probability. The favorable outcomes are 1K, 3K, 1J, 3J (odd ball and K-or-J card), with counts 11 + 9 + 8 + 6 = 34. The total trials is 70.

P(experimental) = 34/70 ≈ 0.486

Step 2. Theoretical probability. Two independent events: ball is odd, card is K or J. By the multiplication rule:

P(theoretical) = P(odd ball) · P(K or J) = (2/3) · (2/3) = 4/9 ≈ 0.444

Step 3. Compare. The experimental value 0.486 is close to the theoretical value 0.444, off by about 0.04. By the Law of Large Numbers, this gap should shrink (in expectation) as the number of trials grows.

Both numbers are valid probabilities, but they answer different questions. The experimental value reports what actually happened in this particular 70-trial run; the theoretical value reports what should happen in any sufficiently long run.

In the wildInsurance actuaries, public-health epidemiologists, and quality-control engineers all rely on the Law of Large Numbers to keep their organizations solvent. A single coin flip is unpredictable; ten thousand coin flips are highly predictable in aggregate. The same principle scales up: a single insurance claim is unpredictable in timing and size; ten thousand claims across the company's book of business have an aggregate behavior the actuary can price against. Casinos depend on the same convergence: every single bet has high variance, but a million bets settled at the same edge produce a remarkably consistent profit margin.
Exercises

content.problem: A coin is flipped 200 times; it lands heads on 109 of the flips. Compute the experimental probability of heads, rounded to the nearest thousandth.
content.answer: 0.545. 109/200 = 0.545. Slightly above the theoretical 0.500; the gap should shrink with more flips.

content.problem: Two fair dice are rolled. Compute the theoretical probability that both show even numbers.
content.answer: 1/4. The two events are independent: P(both even) = (1/2)(1/2) = 1/4 = 0.250.

content.problem: True or false: the Law of Large Numbers guarantees that the experimental and theoretical probabilities will eventually be exactly equal.
content.answer: False. The Law of Large Numbers guarantees convergence, not equality. Even at very large n, a small residual difference is essentially always present.

content.problem: If P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.5, and A and B are independent, find P(A and B).
content.answer: 0.2. By the multiplication rule for independent events: P(A ∩ B) = (0.4)(0.5) = 0.2.

content.problem: A spinner with 5 equal slices (numbered 1 through 5) is spun 80 times and lands on 4 a total of 21 times. Compute the absolute difference |experimental P − theoretical P| for the event “land on 4,” rounded to the nearest thousandth.
content.answer: 0.063. The experimental probability is 21/80 = 0.2625; the theoretical probability is 1/5 = 0.2000; the absolute difference is 0.0625, which rounds to 0.063.

content.problem: A coin is flipped 10 times and lands heads 6 times. Which probability does the value 0.6 represent: the theoretical probability of heads, the experimental probability of heads, or neither?
content.answer: The experimental probability of heads, since 6/10 = 0.6 was computed from the data. The theoretical probability of heads for a fair coin is 0.5, independent of any particular run.

content.problem: An A/B test of two button colors on a checkout page: Group A (5,000 users) has a click-through rate of **0.42**. Group B (5,000 users) has **0.45**. (a) State each rate as a percent. (b) Is the difference experimental or theoretical?
content.answer: (a) Group A: **42%**. Group B: **45%**. (b) Both are **experimental** rates — measured from observed clicks, not computed from theory. To know if the 3-point gap is real or noise, you'd compute a margin of error.

content.problem: A coin is flipped 200 times and lands heads 108 times. (a) What is the experimental probability? (b) What is the theoretical probability for a fair coin? (c) Is the result consistent with a fair coin, or does it suggest the coin is biased?

content.text: More worked examples

content.title: Theoretical vs experimental probability
content.problem: A coin is flipped **100 times** and lands heads **57** times. What is the **theoretical** probability of heads? What is the **experimental** probability from this trial?
content.solution:
**Theoretical** = computed from the structure of the problem. A fair coin has two equally likely outcomes:

**P(heads) theoretical = 1/2 = 0.50** = 50%.

**Experimental** = measured from observed trials.

**P(heads) experimental = 57/100 = 0.57** = 57%.

The two values differ. The theoretical value is fixed by the structure (a fair coin); the experimental value depends on what actually happened in this finite sequence of trials.

content.title: Convergence and the Law of Large Numbers
content.problem: If you flipped the same coin **1,000 times** instead of 100, would you expect the experimental probability of heads to be **closer to** or **farther from** the theoretical 0.50?
content.solution:
**Closer to the theoretical value.**

The Law of Large Numbers says that as the number of trials increases, the experimental probability **converges to** the theoretical probability. Concretely:

• With **100 flips**, getting 57 heads (P̂ = 0.57) is within normal variation for a fair coin — that's 7 percentage points off theoretical.

• With **1,000 flips**, we'd typically expect the proportion to land in roughly the 0.48–0.52 range — much tighter, maybe 1–2 percentage points off theoretical.

• With **1,000,000 flips**, the experimental probability would almost certainly land within **±0.001** of 0.50.

*Important nuance.* The Law of Large Numbers does NOT say that after a string of heads, tails becomes "more likely" (that's the gambler's fallacy). It says the long-run *average* approaches the theoretical value, not that individual outcomes compensate for short-run streaks.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Theoretical probability comes from the structure of the problem. Experimental probability comes from observed data. The Law of Large Numbers links them: as the sample grows, the experimental value converges on the theoretical one. Short-term streaks do not 'self-correct' — the convergence is in the long-run average, not in individual outcomes.

content.title: Why long-run averages matter in product testing
content.content: When TikTok rolls out a new algorithm change, they run an A/B test: half of users see the change, half don't, and the company measures the experimental probability of an outcome (re-open within 24 hours) in each group. Theoretical probability — "if the algorithm is just as good, the rates should be equal" — is what the experimental rates are compared to. The law of large numbers is why the test takes time: with too few users, the experimental rates are too noisy to trust. Same math: short-run experimental values bounce around; long-run experimental values converge to the truth.

content.text: Looking ahead
This section closes the chapter, but probability does not stop at the boundaries of Chapter 6.
6.6
